food_subgroup: Offal and organ meats — fried pork intestine salad

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for Thai crispy pork intestine salad specifically; closest parent 'pork intestine food product' (FOODON_03301710) used as anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (Pig intestine) is the closest defensible parent; no specific code exists for the fried and salad-dressed preparation.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Thai crispy pork intestine salad or fried pork intestine dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 200 g, representing a single-plate portion of crispy pork intestine salad as typically served in a Thai restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Pork small intestine, cooked — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 10397) combined with Thai yam salad dressing component estimation from FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros derived from USDA SR Legacy cooked pork small intestine (NDB 10397 / FDC 169453). Salad dressing components (lime juice, fish sauce, chilli, lemongrass, shallots, toasted rice powder) contribute minor carbohydrate and sodium; values adjusted proportionally to reflect a typical Thai yam preparation at approximately 70% intestine and 30% dressing/herb components. Sodium elevated to reflect fish sauce contribution. FAO/INFOODS SEA table used for regional herb and condiment reference.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed meat and salad dishes — analogous estimate based on cooked pork offal with low-GI dressing components; reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Thai crispy pork intestine salad (Yam Sai Krob). GI estimated at 32 (low) based on the dominant protein-and-fat composition of deep-fried pork intestine, which suppresses glycemic response, combined with a small carbohydrate load from toasted rice powder and lime-fish sauce dressing. Analogous reference: mixed meat dishes with low-GI dressings from Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables. GL per serving = (32 × 6.5 × 2.0) / 100 = 4.2 for a 200 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from fish sauce and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to an estimated sodium content of approximately 520 mg per 100 g (approximately 1040 mg per standard 200 g serving), which represents nearly half the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index but high saturated fat and cholesterol from deep-fried pork intestine
type2_diabetes.guidance: The low glycemic index (estimated 32) and modest carbohydrate content (6.5 g per 100 g) are favourable for blood glucose management, but the high saturated fat (4.9 g per 100 g) and cholesterol (145 mg per 100 g) from deep-fried pork intestine warrant portion control for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, particularly those with concurrent dyslipidaemia.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High cholesterol, saturated fat, and sodium from deep-fried pork offal and fish sauce
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish only occasionally and in small portions, as the combination of high dietary cholesterol (approximately 145 mg per 100 g), saturated fat (4.9 g per 100 g), and sodium (520 mg per 100 g) from deep-fried pork intestine and fish sauce poses a compounded cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Crispy Pork Intestine Salad (Thai: ยำไส้กรอบ, romanized: Yam Sai Krob) is a traditional Thai offal salad dish in which cleaned pork small intestines are deep-fried until golden and crunchy, then tossed in a bold, tangy, and spicy Thai yam dressing made from lime juice, fish sauce, fresh chillies, lemongrass, shallots, galangal, mint leaves, and toasted rice powder. The dish delivers a striking contrast of textures — the shatteringly crisp intestine against the bright, herbaceous dressing — and a complex flavour profile combining sour, salty, spicy, and umami notes characteristic of Thai yam (salad) cuisine. It is commonly served as a shared appetiser or side dish in Thai restaurants and street-food settings across Thailand and in Thai restaurants in Singapore. Nutritionally, the dish is protein-moderate (approximately 14.8 g per 100 g) and fat-rich (approximately 14.2 g per 100 g) due to the deep-frying process and the inherent fat content of pork intestine. Sodium is elevated (approximately 520 mg per 100 g) primarily from fish sauce. The glycemic index is estimated to be low (approximately 32) owing to the dominant protein-fat matrix and modest carbohydrate content. Clinically, the dish is of concern for individuals with hypertension and cardiovascular disease due to its high sodium, saturated fat, and cholesterol content, and should be consumed in moderation by individuals with Type 2 Diabetes who have concurrent dyslipidaemia.

confidence_reason: No exact match for Thai crispy pork intestine salad (Yam Sai Krob) was found in any primary regional source (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition tables). Nutrient values were adapted from USDA SR Legacy cooked pork small intestine data with proportional adjustment for the salad dressing components. GI is an analogous estimate. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. The input record listed food_group as 'Vegetables', which is incorrect for a pork offal dish; corrected to 'Meat and alternatives'. 2. The input food_name 'Crispy Intestine Delight' is a menu-style commercial name; canonical English name corrected to 'Crispy Pork Intestine Salad' with the commercial name retained as an alias. 3. Vitamin A (vitamin_a_mcg_rae) is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entry for cooked pork small intestine and is not estimable from the dressing components with confidence. 4. Folate (folate_mcg_dfe) is -1 as it is not reported in the primary source for this preparation. 5. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the primary source. 6. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the primary source. 7. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the primary source. 8. The dish is not halal as it contains pork. 9. GI is an analogous estimate (low, ~32) based on the protein-fat dominant composition; no direct GI measurement for Yam Sai Krob was found in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature as of the access date. 10. Price data (SGD 25.00) from field survey data is preserved as contextual metadata but is not used as a nutritional source. 11. The cuisine_tags 'Salad', 'BBQ', and 'Fried Rice Vermicelli' from the input are not consistent with this dish and have been disregarded; 'Thai' and 'Salad' are retained as relevant context.
